questions.text: July 2026: a rooftop comfort-cooling unit holding 40 lbs of R-454B is losing charge. Under the EPA's refrigerant-management (ERR) requirements now in force, what applies?

options.t: It is covered: appliances with ≥15 lbs of HFC/substitute refrigerant must be repaired if the annualized leak rate exceeds 10% for comfort cooling
Nothing — leak-repair requirements only apply to ozone-depleting refrigerants like R-22
options.why: That was true before the AIM Act era. As of Jan 1 2026, leak-repair obligations extend to HFCs and substitutes (GWP > 53) at a 15-lb charge threshold — R-454B qualifies.

options.t: It is covered, but the comfort-cooling trigger rate is 30% per year
options.why: 30% is the INDUSTRIAL PROCESS trigger. Comfort cooling is the strictest: 10%.

options.t: It is exempt because the charge is under the 50-lb threshold that applies to all appliances
options.why: 50 lbs was the old ODS-era threshold. The 2024 ERR rule set 15 lbs for HFC-era appliances — a major 2026 exam update.

questions.why: The 2024 ERR rule (leak-repair provisions effective Jan 1 2026) covers appliances with ≥15 lbs of HFC or substitute refrigerant with GWP > 53. Triggers: 10% comfort cooling · 20% commercial refrigeration · 30% industrial process.
questions.ref: EPA Emissions Reduction & Reclamation rule (2024), leak-repair subpart

questions.text: July 2026: a supply house still has brand-new R-410A split-system components on the shelf (manufactured before January 1, 2025). For a new residential install, a contractor may:

options.t: Install them — EPA's May 2026 reconsideration rule removed the January 1, 2026 installation deadline, so pre-2025-built systems may be installed until supplies run out
Not install them — installing new R-410A systems became illegal nationwide on January 1, 2026
options.why: That WAS the original rule — which is exactly why this distractor works. EPA bridged the deadline with enforcement discretion in 2025 and then permanently removed it in the May 2026 reconsideration rule.

options.t: Install them, and OEMs may also resume building new R-410A condensing units while demand lasts
options.why: Half the update swallowed the other half: the manufacture/import prohibition (GWP ≥ 700, from Jan 1 2025) still stands — only the downstream INSTALLATION deadline was removed.

options.t: Not install them, and existing R-410A systems can no longer be recharged either
options.why: Doubly overcorrected: installs of pre-2025-built stock are allowed again, and servicing/recharging the existing installed base was never prohibited.

questions.why: A genuinely 2026 fact: the manufacture/import ban (GWP ≥ 700) from Jan 1 2025 still stands, but EPA's reconsideration final rule (published May 26, 2026) removed the Jan 1 2026 installation cutoff — R-410A systems built from pre-2025 components may be installed until stock runs out. Stay current: the rules moved twice in 18 months.
questions.ref: EPA Technology Transitions reconsideration final rule (May 2026); 40 CFR 84 Subpart B

questions.text: What federal certification do you need to purchase and handle R-32 and R-454B in 2026?

options.t: EPA Section 608 certification — the venting/handling rules extend to substitutes; A2L safety training is expected by employers and suppliers but is not a separate federal card
A new "EPA A2L endorsement" that must be added to your 608 card
options.why: No such federal endorsement exists. A2L training programs (ESCO, OEM, ACCA) are industry requirements, not an EPA certification category.

options.t: None — A2Ls are not ozone-depleting, so Section 608 does not apply to them
options.why: Section 608's prohibitions and sales restrictions reach substitutes, and the 2026 refrigerant-management regime explicitly covers HFC/A2L refrigerants. 608 certification is required.

options.t: Only Universal certification holders may handle A2Ls; Type II is insufficient for split systems
options.why: The Type I/II/III/Universal structure is about equipment category (small appliances / high-pressure / low-pressure), not refrigerant flammability class. Type II covers residential split systems regardless of A2L.

questions.why: Section 608 remains the federal certification for regulated refrigerants including A2L substitutes. Expect employers, distributors and AHJs to also require documented A2L safety training — but that is industry practice layered on top, not a new EPA license.
questions.ref: EPA Section 608 program; ESCO A2L training program

questions.text: Which two distinct regulatory levers does the AIM Act use, and how do they differ?

options.t: A declining production/import allowance phasedown (supply), and sector-specific GWP limits via Technology Transitions (new equipment)
A single nationwide ban on all HFCs effective the same date
options.why: There is no single all-HFC ban date. The AIM Act uses a gradual allowance phasedown PLUS separate sector GWP limits.

options.t: Only an efficiency standard, identical to DOE SEER2 rules
options.why: The AIM Act is about HFC supply and GWP limits, not equipment efficiency ratings (those are DOE).

options.t: Only a servicing ban on existing equipment
options.why: Servicing existing equipment continues. The AIM Act constrains new HFC supply and new-equipment GWP, not repairs.

questions.why: The AIM Act phases down HFC production and import through a declining allowance system (85% cut by 2036) AND, separately, the Technology Transitions rule sets sector-by-sector GWP limits for new equipment. Reclaim and servicing continue as new supply shrinks.
questions.ref: AIM Act §(e) phasedown; Technology Transitions rule (40 CFR 84)

questions.text: As virgin HFC supply tightens under the phasedown, which practice is EPA increasingly emphasizing to keep existing systems running?

options.t: Reclamation — reprocessing recovered refrigerant to AHRI 700 purity so it can re-enter service
Venting old refrigerant to make room for new
options.why: Venting is illegal and the opposite of the goal. The phasedown pushes toward recovery and reclamation, not release.

options.t: Switching all existing systems to propane (R-290) drop-ins
options.why: You cannot drop A3 propane into equipment not listed for it. Reclamation of existing HFCs is the emphasized path.

options.t: Ignoring leaks since the refrigerant will be banned anyway
options.why: Leak repair and reclamation are emphasized precisely because existing systems must keep running on a shrinking virgin supply.

questions.why: With virgin HFC allowances declining, EPA emphasizes reclamation — reprocessing recovered refrigerant to AHRI 700 purity so it can legally re-enter service — to keep the existing installed base running. Recovery and leak repair feed that supply.
questions.ref: AIM Act reclaim provisions; EPA ERR rule

questions.text: Reclaimed refrigerant re-entering the market must meet which purity standard?

options.t: AHRI Standard 700 purity, verified by an EPA-certified reclaimer
Whatever purity the on-site recycling machine achieves
options.why: On-site recycling is a lower, same-owner-reuse level. Reclaim for the open market requires AHRI 700 purity and verification.

options.t: No standard — reclaimed refrigerant can be resold as recovered
options.why: Recovered ≠ reclaimed. Crossing ownership for reuse requires reclamation to AHRI 700, not just recovery.

options.t: Only that it is the correct refrigerant type, with no purity testing
options.why: AHRI 700 sets specific purity limits (moisture, non-condensables, contaminants) that must be verified — type-matching alone is insufficient.

questions.why: Reclaimed refrigerant must be reprocessed to AHRI Standard 700 purity and verified by an EPA-certified reclaimer before it changes ownership for reuse — a stricter bar than on-site recycling.

questions.text: A tech claims that because R-410A can no longer be used in new equipment, existing R-410A rooftop units must be replaced by a federal deadline. Is that correct?

options.t: No — the transition restricts new equipment; existing R-410A systems may be serviced and kept running indefinitely
Yes — all R-410A equipment must be removed from service by a set federal date
options.why: There is no federal "rip-and-replace" deadline for the installed base. The rules target new equipment; existing systems can be serviced.

options.t: Yes, but only in states that adopt California's rules
options.why: The federal transition does not force removal of existing equipment anywhere; servicing the installed base remains legal.

options.t: No, because R-410A was never actually restricted at all
options.why: R-410A (GWP ≥700) IS restricted for NEW residential/light-commercial equipment since Jan 1 2025 — the point is that existing systems are unaffected, not that nothing changed.

questions.why: The manufacture/import restriction (GWP ≥700, from Jan 1 2025) targets new equipment. Existing R-410A systems may be serviced, repaired and recharged indefinitely — there is no federal deadline forcing replacement of the installed base.
questions.ref: EPA Technology Transitions rule; AIM Act phasedown FAQ
